plainMeaning: I bow to Lord Śāntinātha, merely remembering whom calms the fiercest sufferings of all living beings, and who is the very embodiment of the highest virtues.
recitation: smaraṇaṁ yasya sattvānāṁ, tīvra-tāpopaśāntaye | utkṛṣṭa-guṇa-rūpāya, tasmai śrī-śāntaye namaḥ ||

wordByWord.gloss: remembrance, the act of calling to mind
wordByWord.etymology: From √smṛ ('to remember') + -ana; neuter nominative singular. The same word that names this whole class of protective stotra (smaraṇa).
wordByWord.notes: Subject of the relative clause: 'the remembrance of whom...'.

wordByWord.etymology: Relative pronoun yad, genitive singular masculine; correlates with tasmai ('to that one') in the second line.
wordByWord.notes: Sets up the yasya…tasmai relative–correlative frame.

wordByWord.etymology: sattva ('a being, living creature'; lit. 'that which is/exists,' from sat + -tva), genitive plural.
wordByWord.notes: Book reads સત્વાનાં with a single conjunct (sa-tvānāṁ) for sattvānāṁ; preserved verbatim, not normalized.

wordByWord.etymology: Adjective tīvra ('sharp, severe'); here the first member of the compound qualifying tāpa.
wordByWord.notes: Printed as a separate word but reads with the following term as tīvra-tāpa, 'intense burning/affliction.'

wordByWord.gloss: for the calming of the burning (affliction)
wordByWord.etymology: Sandhi of tāpa + upaśānti: tāpa ('heat, torment, suffering,' from √tap) + upaśānti ('pacification, allaying'; upa + √śam), dative singular upaśāntaye expressing purpose ('for the sake of').
wordByWord.notes: Whole phrase: tīvra-tāpa-upaśāntaye, 'for the pacification of fierce affliction.' 'Burning' carries both bodily fever and the heat of saṁsāra.

wordByWord.etymology: Past participle of ut + √kṛṣ ('to draw up, raise'), hence 'drawn upward, pre-eminent.' Qualifies guṇa.

wordByWord.gloss: to the one whose very form is virtue
wordByWord.etymology: guṇa ('quality, virtue') + rūpa ('form, nature, essence'), dative singular masculine guṇa-rūpāya, agreeing with tasmai.
wordByWord.notes: With utkṛṣṭa: 'to him who is constituted of supreme virtues' — rūpa here means essence, not mere appearance.

wordByWord.etymology: Demonstrative tad, dative singular masculine; the correlative answering yasya.
wordByWord.notes: Governs the dative of homage with namaḥ.

wordByWord.gloss: to glorious Śānti(nātha) / to blessed peace
wordByWord.etymology: śrī ('glorious, blessed') + śānti ('peace'; also the name Śāntinātha), dative singular śāntaye.
wordByWord.notes: Deliberately bivalent: 'to revered Śāntinātha' and 'to blessed peace' at once — the hymn's signature name-play, here resolved onto Śānti alone.

wordByWord.etymology: namas ('obeisance,' from √nam, 'to bow'), nominative used indeclinably with the dative of the one saluted.
wordByWord.notes: The closing word of the verse and of the hymn's salutation.

literalTranslation: He, the remembrance of whom is for the pacification of beings' fierce affliction — to that one whose very form is supreme virtue, to that glorious Śānti, homage.
idiomaticTranslation: To the one whose very remembrance stills the burning anguish of all living beings, the one fashioned wholly of the highest virtues — to that blessed Śāntinātha, peace itself, I bow.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This closing stanza seals the hymn, and it does so with a quiet self-reference: a smaraṇa-stotra ends by praising the power of smaraṇa itself. Where the body of the hymn kept Ajita and Śānti in alternation, the benediction now rests on Śānti alone — peace as the final word. The relative–correlative frame (yasya…tasmai) builds suspense: first the effect, 'the remembrance of whom calms beings' fierce burning,' then the cause withheld until the last foot, 'to that glorious Śānti, homage.' Two epithets carry the weight. tīvra-tāpa, 'fierce burning,' names not merely bodily fever but the heat of saṁsāra — affliction, grief, the fever of fear that the whole hymn is recited to quell; its upaśānti comes through mere remembrance (smaraṇa-mātra), not ritual labour. guṇa-rūpa goes further than the usual 'possessed of virtues': the Jina is constituted of virtue, rūpa as essence, so that to remember him is already to touch the qualities one seeks. And śrī-śānti is poised between two readings — 'blessed Śāntinātha' and 'blessed peace' — letting the name dissolve into the thing it names. The verse thus folds the listener's intent and the hymn's fruit into a single final namaḥ.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The stanza states the logic of protective devotion precisely. A Tīrthaṅkara is vītarāga — passionless — and grants no boon by intervention; yet remembrance of his guṇas reorients the devotee's bhāva, and it is that inner turning which pacifies tīvra-tāpa. guṇa-rūpa points to the Jina as the embodiment of infinite qualities (ananta-guṇa) attained at kevala-jñāna; he is venerated not for what he gives but for what he is. The verse thereby restates, in maṅgala form, the hymn's promise that its recitation dissolves affliction — locating the efficacy in smaraṇa and bhāva rather than in any transaction.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Recited as the final salutation, this verse is the reciter's moment to release the petition and simply rest in śānti: having remembered the Jina's virtues throughout the hymn, one offers the closing namaḥ and lets the 'burning' of fear or grief subside into the peace the name itself carries.

crossReferences.text: Sarva-maṅgala-māṅgalyaṁ… (v.43) and Upasargāḥ kṣayaṁ yānti… (v.44) — the companion appended maṅgala stanzas
crossReferences.source: Ajitaśānti Stava, closing Sanskrit verses (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha Panch Pratikraman, vidhi-sahit)

crossReferences.text: '…upasagga-nivāraṇa,' the hymn invoked as remover of affliction (v.38)
Logassa: 'ārugga-bohi-lābhaṁ samāhi-varam uttamaṁ dintu' — Jina-remembrance sought for health and equanimity

sources.title: Ajit Shanti Stotra (Romanized) — full text
sources.consultedFor: Verifying the appended Sanskrit maṅgala verses and the closing 'tasmai śrī-śāntaye namaḥ' salutation within the Ajitaśānti recension.

sources.title: Monier-Williams Sanskrit-English Dictionary (Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon)
sources.consultedFor: Glosses for tāpa ('heat, torment, affliction'), upaśānti ('pacification, allaying'), sattva ('living being'), and namas.

sources.title: Verse-by-verse meaning of Ajit Shanti Stotra (Jain Knowledge / JainGPT)
sources.consultedFor: Cross-checking the devotional sense of the closing benediction and the smaraṇa-fruit ('all suffering destroyed by remembrance') theme.

notes: Verse text preserved verbatim from the book, including સત્વાનાં (single conjunct for sattvānāṁ) and the space in 'તીવ્ર તાપોપશાંતયે,' which reads as one compound (tīvra-tāpa-upaśāntaye). શ્રીશાંતયે is intentionally bivalent — 'glorious Śāntinātha' and 'blessed peace' — and is rendered to keep both senses; a reading as the abstract 'peace' alone is equally defensible. tāpa is glossed as 'burning/affliction' to hold both literal heat (fever, disease) and the metaphorical fever of saṁsāra; manuscripts agree closely on this stanza, and no significant variant was found for v.46.
